title: pkg/ctime: Factor libpod/finished* into a separate package
body:
This removes some boilerplate from the libpod package, so we can focus on container stuff there.  And it gives us a tidy sub-package for focusing on ctime extraction, so we can focus on unit testing and portability of the extraction utility there.

The unit test seems a bit flaky at the moment, with creation times that predate `begin`, at least on my dev box.

comments.body:
I'm seeing the same flake [on Travis][1] that I'd seen locally:

```
ctime_test.go:34: created 2018-07-05 19:44:20.858799425 +0000 UTC is 3023811 nanoseconds before 2018-07-05 19:44:20.861823236 +0000 UTC m=+0.000492159
```

I'll play with this more tonight, but am happy for any suggestions folks have about what's going on there...

[1]: https://travis-ci.org/projectatomic/libpod/jobs/400559295#L1915
A bit of searching found https://stackoverflow.com/questions/14392975/timestamp-accuracy-on-ext4-sub-millsecond as a potential explanation

> A bit of searching found https://stackoverflow.com/questions/14392975/timestamp-accuracy-on-ext4-sub-millsecond as a potential explanation

Hmm.  Using cached times is fine, but I'd have expected the `Now()` call to flush the cache.  And in the error quoted above, the creation time is >3ms before `begin`, which is surprisingly large.  But I don't know how much we care.  Should I just subtract 100ms from `begin` and hope nobody sets `CONFIG_HZ` too low on a quiet system?

All we really want to test is that the file change time is (relatively) close to the time we get from the OS, indicating we're getting sane results. Taking the absolute value of the difference between the two and ensuring it's less than a microsecond or two sounds like a good way of doing that?

> ... ensuring it's less than a microsecond or two...

In the failure quoted above, it was just over 3ms, which is over 1k times your limit ;).  And if the limit is the configurable kernel timer, it's not clear to me how far I should relax the comparison.

More interesting background reading [in this thread][1].  The lack of interpolation is the point I was missing [above][2]: it's time-at-the-last-tick, not value-of-the-last-expensive-time-call.  Golang doesn't give us a way to select `CLOCK_REALTIME_COARSE`, so I've pushed 0ce4123e -> 9eba6632 to give a second of padding to `begin` and (to ensure monotonicity within the larger window) test two sequentially-created files.

[1]: https://groups.google.com/d/msg/linux.kernel/mdeXx2TBYZA/OyP7orFmAQAJ
[2]: https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/pull/1050#issuecomment-402897890

I've pushed 9eba6632 -> 1b9f13f7 fixing a broken `package` declaration and changing the unsupported implementation to use [`ModTime()`][1] (more discussion in the commit message).

[1]: https://golang.org/pkg/os/#FileInfo

[The Travis failure][1] is:

```
package golang.org/x/crypto/ssh/terminal: unrecognized import path "golang.org/x/crypto/ssh/terminal" (https fetch: Get https://golang.org/x/crypto/ssh/terminal?go-get=1: dial tcp 74.125.196.141:443: i/o timeout)
make: *** [.install.gitvalidation]
```

Just a flakey day today ;).

[1]: https://travis-ci.org/projectatomic/libpod/jobs/400903085#L96

Everybody is happy except [the Travis flake][1].  I have another commit locally to drop the 32-/64-bit distinction on Linux (which seems to work locally).  Should I push that here to trigger retests?  Or wait until this lands and file it as a new PR?

[1]: https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/pull/1050#issuecomment-403063802

@wking New PR once this lands is probably better.
Let me restart the failing travis jobs.
